eg a) \wedge (x \vee 
eg b) \wedge (y \leftrightarrow b \vee 
eg x) \wedge (a \vee y) \wedge (x \vee c) \
&\equiv \underbrace{(x \vee 
eg a) \wedge (x \vee 
eg b) \wedge (y \vee 
eg b) \wedge (y \vee x)}_	ext{2-sat clauses} \wedge \underbrace{(
eg y \vee b \vee 
eg x)}_	ext{Horn clause} \wedge \underbrace{(a \vee y) \wedge (x \vee c)}_	ext{2-sat clauses}
\end{aligned}a‚ą®b‚ą®c‚Äč‚Č°sat(x‚ÜĒa‚ą®b)‚ąß(x‚ą®c)‚Č°((x‚ą®¬¨a)‚ąß(x‚ą®¬¨b)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®a‚ą®b))‚ąß(x‚ą®c)‚Č°(x‚ą®¬¨a)‚ąß(x‚ą®¬¨b)‚ąß(a‚ą®b‚ą®¬¨x)‚ąß(x‚ą®c)‚Č°sat(x‚ą®¬¨a)‚ąß(x‚ą®¬¨b)‚ąß(y‚ÜĒb‚ą®¬¨x)‚ąß(a‚ą®y)‚ąß(x‚ą®c)‚Č°2-sat¬†clauses(x‚ą®¬¨a)‚ąß(x‚ą®¬¨b)‚ąß(y‚ą®¬¨b)‚ąß(y‚ą®x)‚Äč‚Äč‚ąßHorn¬†clause(¬¨y‚ą®b‚ą®¬¨x)‚Äč‚Äč‚ąß2-sat¬†clauses(a‚ą®y)‚ąß(x‚ą®c)‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč

This is clearly a polynomial-time reduction.

Dual Horn-sat and 2-sat clauses

The above problem remains NP\mathcal{NP}NP-complete if we consider Dual-horn clauses instead of (normal) Horn clauses (a clause is called Dual-horn, if it has at most one negative literal).

Analogously to the proof above, in order to prove NP\mathcal{NP}NP-hardness we have to express 3-sat clauses with 2 and 3 negative literals in terms of 2-sat and dual-horn clauses. If the clause has 2 negative literals, then:

¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®c‚Č°sat(¬¨x‚ÜĒ¬¨b‚ą®c)‚ąß(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨x)‚Č°(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Źü2-sat¬†clauses‚ąß(x‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®c)‚ŹüDual¬†horn¬†clause‚ąß(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨x)‚Źü2-sat¬†clause egin{aligned}

eg a \vee 
eg b \vee c
&\overset{	ext{sat}}{\equiv} (
eg x \leftrightarrow 
eg b \vee c) \wedge (
eg a \vee 
eg x) \
&\equiv \underbrace{(
eg x \vee b) \wedge (
eg x \vee 
eg c)}_	ext{2-sat clauses}
\wedge \underbrace{(x \vee 
eg b \vee c)}_	ext{Dual horn clause}
\wedge \underbrace{(
eg a \vee 
eg x)}_	ext{2-sat clause}
\end{aligned}¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®c‚Äč‚Č°sat(¬¨x‚ÜĒ¬¨b‚ą®c)‚ąß(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨x)‚Č°2-sat¬†clauses(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Äč‚Äč‚ąßDual¬†horn¬†clause(x‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®c)‚Äč‚Äč‚ąß2-sat¬†clause(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨x)‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč

Normally, in the Tseitin transformation we make the introduced variables equisatisfiable with the corresponding sub-formula. Here, in order to prevent ¬¨x
eg x¬¨x in the horn clause, we make ¬¨x
eg x¬¨x equisatisfiable with ¬¨b‚ą®c
eg b \vee c¬¨b‚ą®c.

If the clause has 3 negative literals, then we use the same trick two times:

¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®¬¨c‚Č°sat(¬¨x‚ÜĒ¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Č°((¬¨x‚ą®a)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(x‚ą®¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b))‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Č°(¬¨x‚ą®a)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®x)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Č°sat(¬¨x‚ą®a)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨y‚ÜĒ¬¨b‚ą®x)‚ąß(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨y)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Č°(¬¨x‚ą®a)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨y‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨y‚ą®¬¨x)‚Źü2-sat¬†clauses‚ąß(y‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®x)‚ŹüDual¬†horn¬†clause‚ąß(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨y)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Źü2-sat¬†clauses egin{aligned}

eg a \vee

eg c
&\overset{	ext{sat}}{\equiv} (

&\equiv ((
eg x \vee a) \wedge (
eg x \vee b) \wedge (x \vee

&\equiv \underbrace{(
eg x \vee a) \wedge (

eg b \vee x)}_	ext{Dual horn clause} \wedge \underbrace{(

\end{aligned}¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®¬¨c‚Äč‚Č°sat(¬¨x‚ÜĒ¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Č°((¬¨x‚ą®a)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(x‚ą®¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b))‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Č°(¬¨x‚ą®a)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®x)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Č°sat(¬¨x‚ą®a)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨y‚ÜĒ¬¨b‚ą®x)‚ąß(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨y)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Č°2-sat¬†clauses(¬¨x‚ą®a)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨y‚ą®b)‚ąß(¬¨y‚ą®¬¨x)‚Äč‚Äč‚ąßDual¬†horn¬†clause(y‚ą®¬¨b‚ą®x)‚Äč‚Äč‚ąß2-sat¬†clauses(¬¨a‚ą®¬¨y)‚ąß(¬¨x‚ą®¬¨c)‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč

documents.title: Constructing Hilbert-style F0 proofs with a simple graph-based notation
documents.content:
There exist different kinds of deductive systems in logic. Every deductive system consists of certain axioms and rules of inference. The problem of constructing a proof in every such system is quite difficult ‚ÄĒ computationally it is NP\mathcal{NP}NP-Hard, since satisfiability can be reduced to it. So it is a good idea to trade-off between logical axioms and inference rules. Hilbert (Frege)-style proof systems are interesting because most of them use several axioms but only one inference rule ‚ÄĒ modus ponens, unlike, for example, Gentzen natural deduction. In other words, Hilbert-style proof systems ‚Äúpush‚ÄĚ all the complexity of constructing a proof into the axioms ‚ÄĒ it is hard to syntactically instantiate them, but once done it is easier to combine them as there is only one rule of inference ‚ÄĒ modus ponens. In this post, I will present a notation for these Hilbert-style proofs I came up with recently, that allows one to construct them in a more systematic way, with less ‚Äúguessing‚ÄĚ.

The F0 Hilbert-style proof system

First, we need to define the proof system in which we will construct proofs. We will consider only formulas over {¬¨,‚Üí}\{\neg, \rightarrow\}{¬¨,‚Üí} without true and false. By the way, this is a complete operator set, so every boolean function can be expressed only by using negation and implication. Let

a‚Üí(b‚Üía)Axiom¬†1(a‚Üí(b‚Üíc))‚Üí((a‚Üíb)‚Üí(a‚Üíc))Axiom¬†2(¬¨a‚Üí¬¨b)‚Üí(b‚Üía)Axiom¬†3\begin{aligned}
a &\rightarrow (b \rightarrow a) & \text{Axiom 1} \\
(a \rightarrow (b \rightarrow c)) &\rightarrow ((a \rightarrow b) \rightarrow (a \rightarrow c)) & \text{Axiom 2} \\
(\neg a \rightarrow \neg b) &\rightarrow (b \rightarrow a) & \text{Axiom 3}
\end{aligned}a(a‚Üí(b‚Üíc))(¬¨a‚Üí¬¨b)‚Äč‚Üí(b‚Üía)‚Üí((a‚Üíb)‚Üí(a‚Üíc))‚Üí(b‚Üía)‚ÄčAxiom¬†1Axiom¬†2Axiom¬†3‚Äč

be the set of axioms, that hold for arbitrary propositional formulas aaa, bbb, and ccc.

The only inference rule we need is modus ponens (MP), which can be defined as follows:

őĪőĪ‚Üíő≤ő≤\begin{array}{c}
\alpha \quad \alpha \rightarrow \beta \\
\hline
\beta
\end{array}őĪőĪ‚Üíő≤ő≤‚Äč‚Äč

It can be proven that this is a sound and complete proof system.

Deduction theorem

In order to considerably simplify the proof construction in many cases, we need to briefly define the deduction theorem, which states that:

ő£‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚áĒő£‚ą™{a}‚äĘb\Sigma \vdash (a \rightarrow b) \Leftrightarrow \Sigma \cup \{a\} \vdash bő£‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚áĒő£‚ą™{a}‚äĘb

This theorem essentially provides a bridge between syntactic entailment and provability in the F0 proof system ‚ÄĒ for example, proving ‚äĘa‚Üíb\vdash a \rightarrow b‚äĘa‚Üíb is equivalent to proving {a}‚äĘb\{a\} \vdash b{a}‚äĘb. Intuitively, this theorem holds because s‚Üí(a‚Üíb)‚ÜĒ(s‚ąßa)‚Üíbs \rightarrow (a \rightarrow b) \leftrightarrow (s \wedge a) \rightarrow bs‚Üí(a‚Üíb)‚ÜĒ(s‚ąßa)‚Üíb is valid and F0 is sound and complete.

The deduction theorem is not a rule of inference or an axiom ‚ÄĒ if some F0 proof uses it, then there exists a proof without it (this can be proven by induction).

Notation for proofs

Usually, proofs are defined as finite sequences of propositional formulas, where each element is either an axiom or modus ponens applied to some already proven formulas (that are located before the current formula in the sequence). For example, a typical proof of ‚äĘa‚Üía\vdash a \rightarrow a‚äĘa‚Üía would be:


  a‚Üí((a‚Üía)‚Üía)a \rightarrow ((a \rightarrow a) \rightarrow a)a‚Üí((a‚Üía)‚Üía) ‚ÄĒ Axiom 1
  (a‚Üí((a‚Üía)‚Üía))‚Üí((a‚Üí(a‚Üía))‚Üí(a‚Üía))(a \rightarrow ((a \rightarrow a) \rightarrow a)) \rightarrow ((a \rightarrow (a \rightarrow a)) \rightarrow (a \rightarrow a))(a‚Üí((a‚Üía)‚Üía))‚Üí((a‚Üí(a‚Üía))‚Üí(a‚Üía)) ‚ÄĒ Axiom 2
  (a‚Üí(a‚Üía))‚Üí(a‚Üía)(a \rightarrow (a \rightarrow a)) \rightarrow (a \rightarrow a)(a‚Üí(a‚Üía))‚Üí(a‚Üía) ‚ÄĒ MP(1, 2)
  a‚Üí(a‚Üía)a \rightarrow (a \rightarrow a)a‚Üí(a‚Üía) ‚ÄĒ Axiom 1
  a‚Üíaa \rightarrow aa‚Üía ‚ÄĒ MP(4, 3)


However, such proofs are hard to construct by hand, because we need to come up with concrete instances of axioms and it isn‚Äôt trivial in many cases which formulas should be substituted for variables. It is therefore much easier to construct the proof in a bottom-up manner, which means that we start with the formula to be proven and then apply axioms, lemmas and modus ponens backwards.

For example, we can rewrite the proof above with the following graph notation:



In this notation:


  Nodes mean proven statements.
  Edge (u,v)‚ąąE(u, v) \in E(u,v)‚ąąE means that statement uuu is needed for the proof of statement vvv in one step ‚ÄĒ application of an axiom/lemma/assumption combined with modus ponens or simply modus ponens.
  Cycles are not allowed, because we cannot refer to the correctness of some statement while proving it (the graph is a DAG).


By the way, this notation is more compact compared to the list-notation because when applying an axiom, lemma or an assumption we don‚Äôt need to state it and then add the result of modus ponens. Instead of that, we just replace the left side of the axiom/lemma/assumption with it‚Äôs right side.

How to come up with F0 proofs

The intuitive ‚Äúalgorithm‚ÄĚ for coming up with proofs in the F0 proof system can be summarized as follows:


  Apply the deduction theorem as much as possible.
  Derive as many formulas as possible from the assumptions.
  If the statement isn‚Äôt already proven, start constructing the proof bottom-up and apply axioms, lemmas, and assumptions as well as modus ponens backwards.


While performing the bottom-up construction of the proof, our goal is essentially to reach an already proven statement or an assumption from the statement to be proven. In order to do so, we need to iteratively search for ancestor nodes of some node vvv that will get us closer to the desired assumptions or lemmas. For the construction of some node uuu such that (u,v)‚ąąE(u, v) \in E(u,v)‚ąąE holds, we have the following options:


  Add an assumption or an already proven statement to the left of vvv (see, for example, the above proof where the first construction step was to add a‚Üí(a‚Üía)a \rightarrow (a \rightarrow a)a‚Üí(a‚Üía) to the left of v=a‚Üíav = a \rightarrow av=a‚Üía). This step is essentially modus ponens applied backwards.
  Let uuu be the left-hand side of some assumption or already proven statement if vvv is an instance of it‚Äôs right-hand side (see, for example, how Axiom 2 was applied above).


Examples

In the following examples, different F0 proofs are visualized with the graph notation described above. It is also a good idea to read these proofs bottom-up, this is how I constructed them.

Lemma 1 (Trivial implication)

‚äĘa‚Üía\vdash a \rightarrow a‚äĘa‚Üía

Proof: The statement follows directly with the deduction theorem. Also, we already proved this statement above without using the deduction theorem.

Lemma 2 (Double negation elimination)

‚äĘ¬¨¬¨a‚Üía\vdash \neg \neg a \rightarrow a‚äĘ¬¨¬¨a‚Üía

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

¬¨¬¨a‚äĘa\neg \neg a \vdash a¬¨¬¨a‚äĘa



Lemma 3 (Introduce double negation)

‚äĘa‚Üí¬¨¬¨a\vdash a \rightarrow \neg \neg a‚äĘa‚Üí¬¨¬¨a

Proof:



Lemma 4 (Double negation implication)

‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí(¬¨¬¨a‚Üí¬¨¬¨b)\vdash (a \rightarrow b) \rightarrow (\neg \neg a \rightarrow \neg \neg b)‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí(¬¨¬¨a‚Üí¬¨¬¨b)

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{a‚Üíb,¬¨¬¨a}‚äĘ¬¨¬¨b\{a \rightarrow b, \neg \neg a \} \vdash \neg \neg b{a‚Üíb,¬¨¬¨a}‚äĘ¬¨¬¨b



Lemma 5 (Implication transitivity 1)

‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí((b‚Üíc)‚Üí(a‚Üíc))\vdash (a \rightarrow b) \rightarrow ((b \rightarrow c) \rightarrow (a \rightarrow c))‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí((b‚Üíc)‚Üí(a‚Üíc))

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{a,a‚Üíb,b‚Üíc}‚äĘc\{a, a \rightarrow b, b \rightarrow c\} \vdash c{a,a‚Üíb,b‚Üíc}‚äĘc



Lemma 6 (Simple implication transitivity)

‚äĘa‚Üí((a‚Üíb)‚Üíb)\vdash a \rightarrow ((a \rightarrow b) \rightarrow b)‚äĘa‚Üí((a‚Üíb)‚Üíb)

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{a,a‚Üíb}‚äĘb\{a, a \rightarrow b\} \vdash b{a,a‚Üíb}‚äĘb



Lemma 7 (Implication transitivity 2)

‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí((c‚Üía)‚Üí(c‚Üíb))\vdash (a \rightarrow b) \rightarrow ((c \rightarrow a) \rightarrow (c \rightarrow b))‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí((c‚Üía)‚Üí(c‚Üíb))

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{c,c‚Üía,a‚Üíb}‚äĘb\{c, c \rightarrow a, a \rightarrow b\} \vdash b{c,c‚Üía,a‚Üíb}‚äĘb



Lemma 8 (Implication from inconsistency)

‚äĘ¬¨b‚Üí(b‚Üía)\vdash \neg b \rightarrow (b \rightarrow a)‚äĘ¬¨b‚Üí(b‚Üía)

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{¬¨b}‚äĘ(b‚Üía)\{\neg b\} \vdash (b \rightarrow a){¬¨b}‚äĘ(b‚Üía)



Lemma 9 (Implication from contradiction)

‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí((a‚Üí¬¨b)‚Üí(a‚Üíc))\vdash (a \rightarrow b) \rightarrow ((a \rightarrow \neg b) \rightarrow (a \rightarrow c))‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí((a‚Üí¬¨b)‚Üí(a‚Üíc))

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{a,a‚Üíb,a‚Üí¬¨b}‚äĘc\{a, a \rightarrow b, a \rightarrow \neg b\} \vdash c{a,a‚Üíb,a‚Üí¬¨b}‚äĘc



Lemma 10

‚äĘ(¬¨a‚Üía)‚Üía\vdash (\neg a \rightarrow a) \rightarrow a‚äĘ(¬¨a‚Üía)‚Üía

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

(¬¨a‚Üía)‚äĘa(\neg a \rightarrow a) \vdash a(¬¨a‚Üía)‚äĘa



Lemma 11 (Contraposition)

‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí(¬¨b‚Üí¬¨a)\vdash (a \rightarrow b) \rightarrow (\neg b \rightarrow \neg a)‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí(¬¨b‚Üí¬¨a)

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{a‚Üíb,¬¨b}‚äĘ¬¨a\{a \rightarrow b, \neg b\} \vdash \neg a{a‚Üíb,¬¨b}‚äĘ¬¨a



Lemma 12 (Elimination of assumptions)

‚äĘ(b‚Üía)‚Üí((¬¨b‚Üía)‚Üía)\vdash (b \rightarrow a) \rightarrow ((\neg b \rightarrow a) \rightarrow a)‚äĘ(b‚Üía)‚Üí((¬¨b‚Üía)‚Üía)

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{b‚Üía,¬¨b‚Üía}‚äĘa\{b \rightarrow a, \neg b \rightarrow a\} \vdash a{b‚Üía,¬¨b‚Üía}‚äĘa



Lemma 13

‚äĘa‚Üí(¬¨b‚Üí¬¨(a‚Üíb))\vdash a \rightarrow (\neg b \rightarrow \neg (a \rightarrow b))‚äĘa‚Üí(¬¨b‚Üí¬¨(a‚Üíb))

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{a,¬¨b}‚äĘ¬¨(a‚Üíb)\{a, \neg b\} \vdash \neg (a \rightarrow b){a,¬¨b}‚äĘ¬¨(a‚Üíb)



Lemma 14

‚äĘ(a‚Üí¬¨a)‚Üí¬¨a\vdash (a \rightarrow \neg a) \rightarrow \neg a‚äĘ(a‚Üí¬¨a)‚Üí¬¨a

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

(a‚Üí¬¨a)‚äĘ¬¨a(a \rightarrow \neg a) \vdash \neg a(a‚Üí¬¨a)‚äĘ¬¨a



Lemma 15 (Proof by contradiction)

‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí((a‚Üí¬¨b)‚Üí¬¨a)\vdash (a \rightarrow b) \rightarrow ((a \rightarrow \neg b) \rightarrow \neg a)‚äĘ(a‚Üíb)‚Üí((a‚Üí¬¨b)‚Üí¬¨a)

Proof: By the deduction theorem, the above statement is equivalent to

{a‚Üíb,a‚Üí¬¨b}‚äĘ¬¨a\{a \rightarrow b, a \rightarrow \neg b\} \vdash \neg a{a‚Üíb,a‚Üí¬¨b}‚äĘ¬¨a

documents.title: Efficient algorithm for finding non-productive rules in context-free grammars
Finding an injective mapping with restrictions for values is NP-Complete
documents.content:
This post is about an interesting problem I recently tried to solve efficiently but ended up proving its NP-completeness. Informally, the problem is to find an injective mapping between two finite sets with certain, statically defined properties for values. For example, if A={a,b,c,d}A = \{a, b, c, d\}A={a,b,c,d} and B={1,2,3,4,5}B = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}B={1,2,3,4,5}, we may want to find an injective mapping f:A‚ÜíBf : A \rightarrow Bf:A‚ÜíB with restrictions f(a)‚ąą{1,3,4,5}f(a) \in \{1, 3, 4, 5\}f(a)‚ąą{1,3,4,5}, f(b)‚ąą{3,4,5}f(b) \in \{3, 4, 5\}f(b)‚ąą{3,4,5}, f(c)‚ąą{1,2,4,5}f(c) \in \{1, 2, 4, 5\}f(c)‚ąą{1,2,4,5}, f(d)‚ąą{1,2,5}f(d) \in \{1, 2, 5\}f(d)‚ąą{1,2,5}, f(e)‚ąą{1,2,3,4}f(e) \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}f(e)‚ąą{1,2,3,4}. I find this problem of particular interest, because its nature is in my opinion very different compared to well-known NP-complete problems proven to be so by Karp. In this post we will visualize and formalize this problem, prove its NP-completeness and, finally, derive some interesting corollaries.

The problem

The above intuitive description of the problem can be equivalently formalized as follows:

Name: Restricted injective mapping

Input: A set AAA as well as a collection S={A1,‚Ä¶,An}S = \{A_1, \dots, A_n\}S={A1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,An‚Äč} of subsets of AAA, i.e. Ai‚äÜAA_i \subseteq AAi‚Äč‚äÜA for all 1‚Č§i‚Č§n1 \le i \le n1‚Č§i‚Č§n.

Question: ‚ąÉ(a1,‚Ä¶,an)‚ąąA1√ó‚čĮ√óAn\exists (a_1, \dots, a_n) \in A_1 \times \dots \times A_n‚ąÉ(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,an‚Äč)‚ąąA1‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚Äč such that ai‚Č†aja_i \neq a_jai‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč=aj‚Äč for all i‚Č†j,1‚Č§i,j‚Č§ni \neq j, 1 \le i,j \le niÓÄ†‚Äč=j,1‚Č§i,j‚Č§n?

The Hamiltonian circuit problem is a well-known NP\mathcal{NP}NP‚Äč-complete problem, which is formally defined as follows:

Input: Undirected graph G=(V,E)G = (V, E)G=(V,E)

If we prove NP\mathcal{NP}NP-hardness of HC with a reduction fff that cannot produce graphs with two or more vertex-disjoint cycles such that every node is in exactly one such cycle, and then prove NP\mathcal{NP}NP-hardness of Restricted bijective mapping with a reduction ggg that assumes that the input graph cannot be partitioned in vertex-disjoint cycles as mentioned above, then the composite reduction g‚ąėfg \circ fg‚ąėf will prove NP\mathcal{NP}NP-hardness of our problem. For a formal proof I will therefore need to reiterate a variant of the well-known proof of NP\mathcal{NP}NP‚Äč-completeness of HC emphasizing the fact that only graphs with the above mentioned constraint are being produced.

In order to prove the correctness of the present reduction, we therefore need to argue why GGG has a hamiltonian circuit if and only if:

‚ąÉ(a1,‚Ä¶,an)‚ąąA1√ó‚čĮ√óAn:‚ąÄ1‚Č§i,j‚Č§n:i‚Č†j‚áíai‚Č†aj\exists (a_1, \dots, a_n) \in A_1 \times \dots \times A_n : \forall 1 \le i, j \le n : i \neq j \Rightarrow a_i \neq a_j‚ąÉ(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,an‚Äč)‚ąąA1‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚Äč:‚ąÄ1‚Č§i,j‚Č§n:iÓÄ†‚Äč=j‚áíai‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč=aj‚Äč

(‚áí\Rightarrow‚áí‚Äč‚Äč): Assuming there exists a hamiltonian circuit (p1,‚Ä¶,pn,p1)‚ąąVn+1(p_1, \dots, p_n, p_1) \in V^{n+1}(p1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,pn‚Äč,p1‚Äč)‚ąąVn+1, we can choose

(apn,ap1,‚Ä¶,apn‚ąí1)=(p1,p2,‚Ä¶,pn)‚ąąApn√óAp1√ó‚čĮ√óApn‚ąí1(a_{p_n}, a_{p_1}, \dots, a_{p_{n-1}}) = (p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n) \in A_{p_n} \times A_{p_1} \times \dots \times A_{p_{n-1}}(apn‚Äč‚Äč,ap1‚Äč‚Äč,‚Ä¶,apn‚ąí1‚Äč‚Äč)=(p1‚Äč,p2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,pn‚Äč)‚ąąApn‚Äč‚Äč√óAp1‚Äč‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óApn‚ąí1‚Äč‚Äč

nnn distinct values from A1√ó‚čĮ√óAnA_1 \times \dots \times A_nA1‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚Äč.

(‚áź\Leftarrow‚áź‚Äč‚Äč): Let (a1,‚Ä¶,an)‚ąąA1√ó‚čĮ√óAn(a_1, \dots, a_n) \in A_1 \times \dots \times A_n(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,an‚Äč)‚ąąA1‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč such that ai‚Č†aja_i \neq a_jai‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč=aj‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč for i‚Č†ji \neq jiÓÄ†‚Äč=j‚Äč‚Äč. We can define the following mapping:

Ōą:V‚ÜíVx‚Ü¶ax\begin{aligned}
\psi : V &\rightarrow V \\
x &\mapsto a_x
\end{aligned}Ōą:Vx‚Äč‚ÜíV‚Ü¶ax‚Äč‚Äč

It follows from the fact that aia_iai‚Äč‚Äč values are pairwise distinct, that Ōą\psiŌą‚Äč is injective. Moreover, Ōą\psiŌą‚Äč is bijective as the set of vertices VVV‚Äč‚Äč is finite. As we argued in the np-completeness of hc section, GGG cannot be partitioned into more than one vertex-disjoint cycle. This means that:

‚ąÄv‚ąąV,k‚ąąN0:k<n‚áíŌąk(v)‚Č†v\forall v \in V, k \in \mathbb{N}_0 : k < n \Rightarrow\psi^k(v) \neq v‚ąÄv‚ąąV,k‚ąąN0‚Äč:k<n‚áíŌąk(v)ÓÄ†‚Äč=v

However, because Ōą\psiŌą‚Äč is bijective and each application of Ōą\psiŌą leads to a new value in VVV, it holds that:

‚ąÄv‚ąąV‚ąÉk‚ąąN:1‚Č§k‚Č§n‚ąßŌąk(v)=v\forall v \in V \exists k \in \mathbb{N} : 1 \le k \le n \wedge \psi^k(v) = v‚ąÄv‚ąąV‚ąÉk‚ąąN:1‚Č§k‚Č§n‚ąßŌąk(v)=v

It follows from the above two statements that

‚ąÄv‚ąąV:Ōąn(v)=v\forall v \in V : \psi^n(v) = v‚ąÄv‚ąąV:Ōąn(v)=v

and nnn is the minimal number of times Ōą\psiŌą must be applied to reach vvv starting in vvv‚Äč. GGG therefore contains

{1,Ōą(1)}‚ąąE{Ōą(1),Ōą(Ōą(1))}‚ąąE{Ōą(Ōą(1)),Ōą(Ōą(Ōą(1)))}‚ąąE‚čģ{Ōąn‚ąí2(1),Ōąn‚ąí1(1)}‚ąąE{Ōąn‚ąí1(1),Ōąn(1)‚Źü1}‚ąąE\begin{aligned}
\{1, \psi(1)\} &\in E \\
\{\psi(1), \psi(\psi(1))\} &\in E \\
\{\psi(\psi(1)), \psi(\psi(\psi(1)))\} &\in E \\
\vdots \\
\{\psi^{n-2}(1), \psi^{n-1}(1)\} &\in E \\
\{\psi^{n-1}(1), \underbrace{\psi^n(1)}_{1}\} &\in E
\end{aligned}{1,Ōą(1)}{Ōą(1),Ōą(Ōą(1))}{Ōą(Ōą(1)),Ōą(Ōą(Ōą(1)))}‚čģ{Ōąn‚ąí2(1),Ōąn‚ąí1(1)}{Ōąn‚ąí1(1),1Ōąn(1)‚Äč‚Äč}‚Äč‚ąąE‚ąąE‚ąąE‚ąąE‚ąąE‚Äč

meaning that there exists a hamiltonian circuit

(1,Ōą(1),‚Ä¶,Ōąn‚ąí1(1),1)(1, \psi(1), \dots, \psi^{n-1}(1), 1)(1,Ōą(1),‚Ä¶,Ōąn‚ąí1(1),1)

This completes the proof.

Corollaries

NP-completeness of finding an injective mapping

Restricted injective mapping is obviously in NP\mathcal{NP}NP‚Äč.

The NP\mathcal{NP}NP‚Äč-hardness of the present problem follows directly from the NP\mathcal{NP}NP-hardness of Restricted bijective mapping as we can construct a trivial reduction from the latter problem that just passes the instance without any changes.

Counting the amount of injective mappings is NP-hard

Consider an instance of Restricted injective mapping, concretely a set AAA and a collection S={A1,‚Ä¶,An}S = \{A_1, \dots, A_n\}S={A1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,An‚Äč} of subsets of AAA. It may be interesting not only to determine whether we can choose (a1,‚Ä¶,an)‚ąąA1√ó‚čĮ√óAn(a_1, \dots, a_n) \in A_1 \times \dots \times A_n(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,an‚Äč)‚ąąA1‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚Äč such that ai‚Č†aja_i \neq a_jai‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč=aj‚Äč for all i‚Č†j,1‚Č§i,j‚Č§ni \neq j, 1 \le i,j \le niÓÄ†‚Äč=j,1‚Č§i,j‚Č§n, but also to count the amount of such possibilities and to assess the hardness of this combinational problem. With the inclusion‚Äďexclusion principle we can count the amount of possibilities of choosing (a1,‚Ä¶,an)‚ąČA1√ó‚čĮ√óAn(a_1, \dots, a_n) \notin A_1 \times \dots \times A_n(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,an‚Äč)‚ąą/‚ÄčA1‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚Äč such that ai‚Č†aja_i \neq a_jai‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč=aj‚Äč for all i‚Č†j,1‚Č§i,j‚Č§ni \neq j, 1 \le i,j \le niÓÄ†‚Äč=j,1‚Č§i,j‚Č§n‚Äč‚Äč. Let Ai‚Äĺ:=A‚ąĖAi\overline{A_i} := A \setminus A_iAi‚Äč‚Äč:=A‚ąĖAi‚Äč. To do that, we first define:

Mi,u:={(a1,‚Ä¶,an)‚ąČA1√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚ą£ai=u}M_{i,u} := \{(a_1, \dots, a_n) \notin A_1 \times \dots \times A_n \mid a_i = u\}Mi,u‚Äč:={(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,an‚Äč)‚ąą/‚ÄčA1‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚Äč‚ą£ai‚Äč=u}

The above amount of possibilities of choosing the wrong tuples that still satisfy the condition ai‚Č†aja_i \neq a_jai‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč=aj‚Äč for i‚Č†ji \neq jiÓÄ†‚Äč=j is equal to:

ő≥:=‚ą£(‚čÉu‚ąąA1‚ÄĺM1,u)‚ą™‚čĮ‚ą™(‚čÉu‚ąąAn‚ÄĺMn,u)‚ą£\gamma := \Bigg\vert \Bigg(\bigcup_{u \in \overline{A_1}} M_{1, u}\Bigg) \cup \dots \cup \Bigg(\bigcup_{u \in \overline{A_n}} M_{n, u}\Bigg)\Bigg\vertő≥:=‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚Äč(u‚ąąA1‚Äč‚Äč‚čÉ‚ÄčM1,u‚Äč)‚ą™‚čĮ‚ą™(u‚ąąAn‚Äč‚Äč‚čÉ‚ÄčMn,u‚Äč)‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚Äč

This formula has O(n‚čÖ‚ą£A‚ą£)O(n \cdot \st A \st)O(n‚čÖ‚ą£A‚ą£)‚Äč terms and, given the value of ő≥\gammaő≥‚Äč‚Äč, we can calculate the amount of possibilities we are interested in efficiently using the following formula:

(‚ąŹi=0n‚ąí1(‚ą£A‚ą£‚ąíi))‚ąíő≥\Bigg(\prod_{i=0}^{n - 1} (\st A \st - i)\Bigg) - \gamma(i=0‚ąŹn‚ąí1‚Äč(‚ą£A‚ą£‚ąíi))‚ąíő≥

For these reasons, the calculation of ő≥\gammaő≥‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč is the performance bottleneck of this problem. We can calculate ő≥\gammaő≥ ‚Äčusing

ő≥=‚ąĎk=1‚ą£I‚ą£(‚ąí1)k‚ąí1‚ąĎT‚äÜI‚ą£T‚ą£=k‚ą£‚čāt‚ąąTMt‚ą£\gamma = \sum_{k=1}^{\st I \st} (-1)^{k-1}\sum_{\substack{T \subseteq I \\ \st T \st = k}} \Big\vert \bigcap_{t \in T} M_t \Big\vertő≥=k=1‚ąĎ‚ą£I‚ą£‚Äč(‚ąí1)k‚ąí1T‚äÜI‚ą£T‚ą£=k‚Äč‚ąĎ‚Äč‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚Äčt‚ąąT‚čā‚ÄčMt‚Äč‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚ą£‚Äč

where III‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč is the set of indices for the set MMM‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč that come up in the formula for ő≥\gammaő≥‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč, but this approach would unfortunately cause exponential blowup. The key question I now want to address is whether it may still be somehow possible to calculate ő≥\gammaő≥‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč more efficiently.

The problem

Name: Restricted injective mapping count

Input: A set AAA, collection S={A1,‚Ä¶,An}S = \{A_1, \dots, A_n\}S={A1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,An‚Äč} of subsets of AAA and k‚ąąN0k \in \mathbb{N}_0k‚ąąN0‚Äč.

Question: Is the amount of possibilities of choosing (a1,‚Ä¶,an)‚ąąA1√ó‚čĮ√óAn(a_1, \dots, a_n) \in A_1 \times \dots \times A_n(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,an‚Äč)‚ąąA1‚Äč√ó‚čĮ√óAn‚Äč such that ai‚Č†aja_i \neq a_jai‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč=aj‚Äč for all i‚Č†j,1‚Č§i,j‚Č§ni \neq j, 1 \le i,j \le niÓÄ†‚Äč=j,1‚Č§i,j‚Č§n‚Äč at least kkk?

is NP\mathcal{NP}NP-hard, since Restricted injective mapping can be reduced to it (with a reduction that simply sets k:=1k := 1k:=1 and passes the instance of the problem without any changes). We can therefore conclude, that ő≥\gammaő≥ cannot be computed efficiently unless P=NP\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}P=NP‚Äč. Intuitively, the present combinational problem uses the full power of the inclusion-exclusion principle (see this post for similar reasoning).

documents.title: Visualization and detailed description of the interactive protocol for TQBF yielding IP = PSPACE
documents.content:
In complexity theory, it is well known that IP = PSPACE. In this post I would like to go over the private coin interactive protocol for TQBF typically used to prove that PSPACE is contained in IP and, in that context, present my detailed visualization of the entire protocol together with the Open Source tool I developed to run the protocol and render the animations. I would also like to note that when I was first studying the proof protocol for TQBF, I was a bit frustrated by the fact that most texts lack a precise and detailed description of some subtleties in the proof ‚ÄĒ this is what also motivated me to try my best to fill in the relevant but missing details in this post.

Shamir‚Äôs Theorem

In 1990, Adi Shamir proved that IP‚Ā°=PSPACE‚Ā°\operatorname{IP} = \operatorname{PSPACE}IP=PSPACE. I find this result quite surprising, because it shows that just allowing the verifier to toss coins increases the computational power of polynomially long lasting interactive proof systems drastically, namely from dIP‚Ā°=NP‚Ā°\operatorname{dIP} = \operatorname{NP}dIP=NP to PSPACE‚Ā°\operatorname{PSPACE}PSPACE. It is not hard to see why IP‚Ā°‚äÜPSPACE‚Ā°\operatorname{IP} \subseteq \operatorname{PSPACE}IP‚äÜPSPACE ‚ÄĒ this is the case because we can try running the interactive proof system for all possible random bits the verifier may use and messages the prover could potentially send. After that, it is possible to check whether we were able to find a way to convince the verifier for most possibilities of choosing random bits. More precisely, a PSPACE‚Ā°\operatorname{PSPACE}PSPACE decider for L‚ąąIP‚Ā°L \in \operatorname{IP}L‚ąąIP can be constructed as follows (pseudocode):

c = 0
threshold = ceil(2/3 * (total number of different ways the verifier can toss coins))
for all possible verifier's random bits {
    for all possible message sequences the prover may send during the communication {
        Run the verifier using the current message sequence and random bits;
        if verifier accepted {
            c++;
            break;
        }
    }
}
if c >= threshold {
    accept;
} else {
    reject;
}


So the main challenge of showing IP‚Ā°=PSPACE‚Ā°\operatorname{IP} = \operatorname{PSPACE}IP=PSPACE is actually showing that PSPACE‚Ā°‚äÜIP‚Ā°\operatorname{PSPACE} \subseteq \operatorname{IP}PSPACE‚äÜIP. Since IP‚Ā°\operatorname{IP}IP is closed under polynomial time Karp reductions and TQBF‚Ā°\operatorname{TQBF}TQBF is PSPACE‚Ā°\operatorname{PSPACE}PSPACE-complete, it suffices to show that TQBF‚Ā°‚ąąIP‚Ā°\operatorname{TQBF} \in \operatorname{IP}TQBF‚ąąIP. There are multiple ways of proving it ‚ÄĒ I will generally follow Shamir‚Äôs approach and use a trick called linearization, which was introduced by Shen in this paper.

Arithmetization

The main tool used in the proof is arithmetization, that is, the representation of QBF sentences using polynomials. Assuming without loss of generality that the given quantified boolean formula

ŌÜ=Q1x1‚Ä¶QnxnŌÜ‚Ä≤(x1,‚Ä¶,xn),Qi‚ąą{‚ąÉ,‚ąÄ}\varphi = \mathcal{Q}_1 x_1 \dots \mathcal{Q}_n x_n \varphi'(x_1, \dots, x_n), \quad\mathcal{Q}_i \in \{\exists, \forall\}ŌÜ=Q1‚Äčx1‚Äč‚Ä¶Qn‚Äčxn‚ÄčŌÜ‚Ä≤(x1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,xn‚Äč),Qi‚Äč‚ąą{‚ąÉ,‚ąÄ}

is in prenex normal form with matrix ŌÜ‚Ä≤\varphi'ŌÜ‚Ä≤ in CNF, we can obtain a polynomial PŌÜP_{\varphi}PŌÜ‚Äč by essentially thinking of disjunctions as negated conjunctions and by simulating conjunctions with multiplication. Formally, if xix_ixi‚Äč is an arbitrary variable, lijl_{i_j}lij‚Äč‚Äč is an arbitrary literal and CiC_iCi‚Äč is an arbitrary clause appearing in ŌÜ‚Ä≤=C1‚ąß‚Ä¶Cm\varphi' = C_1 \wedge \dots C_mŌÜ‚Ä≤=C1‚Äč‚ąß‚Ä¶Cm‚Äč, then we can define the arithmetizing polynomial PŌÜ:=P(ŌÜ‚Ä≤)P_{\varphi} := P(\varphi')PŌÜ‚Äč:=P(ŌÜ‚Ä≤) recursively as follows:

P(xi):=1‚ąíxiP(xi‚Äĺ):=xiP(li1‚ą®‚čĮ‚ą®lik):=1‚ąíP(li1)‚čÖ‚čĮ‚čÖP(lik)P(C1‚ąß‚Ä¶Cm):=P(C1)‚čÖ‚čĮ‚čÖP(Cm)\begin{aligned}
P(x_i) &:= 1 - x_i \\
P(\overline{x_i}) &:= x_i \\
P(l_{i_1} \vee \dots \vee l_{i_k}) &:= 1 - P(l_{i_1}) \cdot \dots \cdot P(l_{i_k}) \\
P(C_1 \wedge \dots C_m) &:= P(C_1) \cdot \dots \cdot P(C_m)
\end{aligned}P(xi‚Äč)P(xi‚Äč‚Äč)P(li1‚Äč‚Äč‚ą®‚čĮ‚ą®lik‚Äč‚Äč)P(C1‚Äč‚ąß‚Ä¶Cm‚Äč)‚Äč:=1‚ąíxi‚Äč:=xi‚Äč:=1‚ąíP(li1‚Äč‚Äč)‚čÖ‚čĮ‚čÖP(lik‚Äč‚Äč):=P(C1‚Äč)‚čÖ‚čĮ‚čÖP(Cm‚Äč)‚Äč

By definition of PŌÜP_{\varphi}PŌÜ‚Äč and the above discussion, for any values b1,‚Ä¶,bn‚ąą{0,1}b_1, \dots, b_n \in \{0, 1\}b1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,bn‚Äč‚ąą{0,1} it holds that

PŌÜ(b1,‚Ä¶,bn)=1‚áĒŌÜ‚Ä≤(b1,‚Ä¶,bn)=1P_\varphi(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 1 \Leftrightarrow \varphi'(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 1PŌÜ‚Äč(b1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,bn‚Äč)=1‚áĒŌÜ‚Ä≤(b1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,bn‚Äč)=1

Now we need to handle quantifiers. In this connection, note that for some boolean formula Ōą(x)\psi(x)Ōą(x), ‚ąÉx:Ōą(x)\exists x :\psi(x)‚ąÉx:Ōą(x) or ‚ąÄx:Ōą(x)\forall x : \psi(x)‚ąÄx:Ōą(x) make the sentence evaluate to true if and only if Ōą(0)‚ą®Ōą(1)\psi(0) \vee \psi(1)Ōą(0)‚ą®Ōą(1) or Ōą(0)‚ąßŌą(1)\psi(0) \wedge \psi(1)Ōą(0)‚ąßŌą(1) hold, respectively. This property remains true if Ōą\psiŌą contains more variables than just xxx. We can simulate the disjunction Ōą(0)‚ą®Ōą(1)\psi(0) \vee \psi(1)Ōą(0)‚ą®Ōą(1) with a sum of arithmetizations of Ōą(0)\psi(0)Ōą(0) and Ōą(1)\psi(1)Ōą(1), wheras Ōą(0)‚ąßŌą(1)\psi(0) \wedge \psi(1)Ōą(0)‚ąßŌą(1) can be similarly arithmetized to a product. To sum up, we can arithmetize a given quantified boolean sentence ŌÜ=Q1x1‚Ä¶QnxnŌÜ‚Ä≤(x1,‚Ä¶,xn)\varphi = \mathcal{Q}_1 x_1 \dots \mathcal{Q}_n x_n \varphi'(x_1, \dots, x_n)ŌÜ=Q1‚Äčx1‚Äč‚Ä¶Qn‚Äčxn‚ÄčŌÜ‚Ä≤(x1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,xn‚Äč) as follows:


  Replace the matrix ŌÜ‚Ä≤\varphi'ŌÜ‚Ä≤ with PŌÜ(x1,‚Ä¶,xn)P_{\varphi}(x_1, \dots, x_n)PŌÜ‚Äč(x1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,xn‚Äč).
  Replace ‚ąÉxi\exists x_i‚ąÉxi‚Äč with ‚ąĎbi=01\sum_{b_i=0}^1‚ąĎbi‚Äč=01‚Äč and substitute bib_ibi‚Äč for xix_ixi‚Äč in PŌÜ(x1,‚Ä¶,xn)P_{\varphi}(x_1, \dots, x_n)PŌÜ‚Äč(x1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,xn‚Äč).
  Replace ‚ąÄxi\forall x_i‚ąÄxi‚Äč with ‚ąŹbi=01\prod_{b_i=0}^1‚ąŹbi‚Äč=01‚Äč and substitute bib_ibi‚Äč for xix_ixi‚Äč in PŌÜ(x1,‚Ä¶,xn)P_{\varphi}(x_1, \dots, x_n)PŌÜ‚Äč(x1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,xn‚Äč).


More formally, while considering the quantifiers from right to left we have the invariant that the value of the polynomial considered so far is 0 iff the formula is false. Taking this invariant as an inductive hypothesis, we can easily see that ‚ąĎ\sum‚ąĎ and ‚ąŹ\prod‚ąŹ provide the semantically correct simulation of ‚ąÉ\exists‚ąÉ and ‚ąÄ\forall‚ąÄ, respectively, in the inductive step. Using the notation ‚®Ä‚ąą{‚ąĎ,‚ąŹ}\bigodot \in \{\sum, \prod\}‚®Ä‚ąą{‚ąĎ,‚ąŹ} to denote the correct operation needed to simulate the current quantifier, we conclude that:

ŌÜ‚ąąTQBF‚Ā°‚áĒ‚®Äb1=01‚®Äb2=01‚čĮ‚®Äbn=01PŌÜ(b1,b2,‚Ä¶,bn)‚Č†0\varphi \in \operatorname{TQBF} \Leftrightarrow \bigodot_{b_1=0}^1 \bigodot_{b_2=0}^1 \cdots \bigodot_{b_n=0}^1 P_{\varphi}(b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n) \neq 0ŌÜ‚ąąTQBF‚áĒb1‚Äč=0‚®Ä1‚Äčb2‚Äč=0‚®Ä1‚Äč‚čĮbn‚Äč=0‚®Ä1‚ÄčPŌÜ‚Äč(b1‚Äč,b2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,bn‚Äč)ÓÄ†‚Äč=0

For example, the QBF

ŌÜ=‚ąÉx‚ąÄy‚ąÉz‚ąÄw:(x‚ą®y‚ą®z‚Äĺ)‚ąß(x‚Äĺ‚ą®y‚Äĺ‚ą®w‚Äĺ)‚ąß(y‚Äĺ‚ą®z‚ą®w)\varphi = \exists x \forall y \exists z \forall w : (x \vee y \vee \overline{z}) \wedge (\overline{x} \vee \overline{y} \vee \overline{w}) \wedge (\overline{y} \vee z \vee w)ŌÜ=‚ąÉx‚ąÄy‚ąÉz‚ąÄw:(x‚ą®y‚ą®z)‚ąß(x‚ą®y‚Äč‚ą®w)‚ąß(y‚Äč‚ą®z‚ą®w)

would get arithmetized as follows:





As a side note, I generated this animation using this tool I developed recently. At the end of the above visualization, we get:



Refinement of arithmetization

Before we can make use of the representation of QBF‚Äôs as polynomials in the interactive proof protocol, we need to ensure that they satisfy some ‚Äúnice properties‚ÄĚ. In this section, I will define such properties and show how we can ensure that they indeed hold. To make the notation introduced above more robust and easier to work with, we can first define the following functions that take a multivariate polynomial ppp as input and produce another polynomial:

Linearization

For the polynomial-time verifier to be able to work properly with polynomials, we (will) need to ensure that they have low degree, i.e., the degree must itself be bounded by a polynomial in the size of ŌÜ\varphiŌÜ. Note that the degree of PŌÜP_{\varphi}PŌÜ‚Äč is low, because deg‚Ā°(PŌÜ)‚Č§n‚čÖm\deg(P_{\varphi}) \le n \cdot mdeg(PŌÜ‚Äč)‚Č§n‚čÖm, where mmm is the amount of clauses. While ‚ąÉxi\exists_{x_i}‚ąÉxi‚Äč‚Äč adds polynomials which doesn‚Äôt increase their degree, ‚ąÄxi\forall_{x_i}‚ąÄxi‚Äč‚Äč multiplies polynomials meaning that the degree can get doubled in the worst case. If this happens in every step, then the degree of the entire arithmetization may get exponential, which is unacceptable. To resolve this issue, we can introduce a linearity operator LxiL_{x_i}Lxi‚Äč‚Äč defined as follows:

L_{x_i}(p)(x_1, \dots, x_n) &:= x_i \cdot p(x_1, \dots, x_{i-1}, 1, x_{i+1}, \dots, x_n) \\

gives us xy+yx2+x3xy + yx^2 + x^3xy+yx2+x3 which is linear in yyy. The correctness of the linearity operator follows from the fact that v2=vv^2 = vv2=v for all v‚ąą{0,1}v \in \{0, 1\}v‚ąą{0,1}. In the previous section, we‚Äôve ensured that the intermediate polynomials all have low degree. However, there is another problem ‚ÄĒ we still may not be able to evaluate such low degree polynomials in polynomial time, because the value can get exponentially large with respect to the previous value, at every proof operator, meaning that the tight upper bound for the value of the entire polynomial is O(2(2n))O(2^{(2^n)})O(2(2n)). We can resolve this issue by picking a prime number ppp and doing all computations modulo ppp, that is, in the Fp\mathbb{F}_pFp‚Äč field. &Q_{x_1} Q_{x_2} \dots Q_{x_n} P_{\varphi} \neq 0 \Leftrightarrow \\
&\Leftrightarrow\text{There is a prime } p \in O(2^{n^d}) \text{ such that } Q_{x_1} Q_{x_2} \dots Q_{x_n} P_{\varphi} \not\equiv 0 \mod p
\end{aligned}‚ÄčQx1‚Äč‚ÄčQx2‚Äč‚Äč‚Ä¶Qxn‚Äč‚ÄčPŌÜ‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč=0‚áĒ‚áĒThere¬†is¬†a¬†prime¬†p‚ąąO(2nd)¬†such¬†that¬†Qx1‚Äč‚ÄčQx2‚Äč‚Äč‚Ä¶Qxn‚Äč‚ÄčPŌÜ‚ÄčÓÄ†‚Äč‚Č°0modp‚Äč

  Verifier: If Qx1=‚ąÉx1Q_{x_1} = \exists_{x_1}Qx1‚Äč‚Äč=‚ąÉx1‚Äč‚Äč, verify that s(0)+s(1)=cs(0) + s(1) = cs(0)+s(1)=c, if Qx1=‚ąÄx1Q_{x_1} = \forall_{x_1}Qx1‚Äč‚Äč=‚ąÄx1‚Äč‚Äč, verify that s(0)‚čÖs(1)=cs(0) \cdot s(1) = cs(0)‚čÖs(1)=c and if Qx1=Lx1Q_{x_1} = L_{x_1}Qx1‚Äč‚Äč=Lx1‚Äč‚Äč, verify that a1‚čÖs(1)+(1‚ąía1)‚čÖs(0)=ca_1 \cdot s(1) + (1 - a_1) \cdot s(0) = ca1‚Äč‚čÖs(1)+(1‚ąía1‚Äč)‚čÖs(0)=c. If one of these checks failed, reject.
  Verifier: Pick a‚ąąFpa \in \mathbb{F}_pa‚ąąFp‚Äč uniformly at random and re-run the same protocol to check that s(a)=g‚Ä≤(a,a2,‚Ä¶,ak)s(a) = g'(a, a_2, \dots, a_k)s(a)=g‚Ä≤(a,a2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ak‚Äč).


On top of this protocol, it is now easy to build the interactive proof protocol for TQBF‚Ā°\operatorname{TQBF}TQBF:


  Verifier: Ask the prover to send the value of the entire proof operator polynomial.
  Prover: Sends c‚ąąFpc \in \mathbb{F}_pc‚ąąFp‚Äč,
  Verifier: If c=0c = 0c=0, reject (because this means that the prover directly confessed that the value of the polynomial is 0 meaning that ŌÜ‚ąČTQBF‚Ā°\varphi \notin \operatorname{TQBF}ŌÜ‚ąą/‚ÄčTQBF).
  Verifier: Using the operator composition check protocol defined above, verify that


Qx1Lx1Qx2Lx1Lx2Qx3Lx1Lx2Lx3‚Ä¶Qxn‚ąí1Lx1‚Ä¶Lxn‚ąí1QxnLx1‚Ä¶LxnPŌÜ=cQ_{x_1} L_{x_1} Q_{x_2} L_{x_1} L_{x_2} Q_{x_3} L_{x_1} L_{x_2} L_{x_3} \dots Q_{x_{n-1}} L_{x_1} \dots L_{x_{n-1}} Q_{x_n} L_{x_1} \dots L_{x_n} P_{\varphi} = cQx1‚Äč‚ÄčLx1‚Äč‚ÄčQx2‚Äč‚ÄčLx1‚Äč‚ÄčLx2‚Äč‚ÄčQx3‚Äč‚ÄčLx1‚Äč‚ÄčLx2‚Äč‚ÄčLx3‚Äč‚Äč‚Ä¶Qxn‚ąí1‚Äč‚ÄčLx1‚Äč‚Äč‚Ä¶Lxn‚ąí1‚Äč‚ÄčQxn‚Äč‚ÄčLx1‚Äč‚Äč‚Ä¶Lxn‚Äč‚ÄčPŌÜ‚Äč=c

Example visualized

In this section I would like to present my tool that allows us to both obtain the interactive transcript in text format, as well as render a video animation showing in detail every aspect of the protocol. The implemented protocol is exactly the protocol defined in this post, all notations are also consistent. In the arithmetization section above, we have already arithmetized a concrete formula, namely

ŌÜ=‚ąÉx‚ąÄy‚ąÉz‚ąÄw:(x‚ą®y‚ą®z‚Äĺ)‚ąß(x‚Äĺ‚ą®y‚Äĺ‚ą®w‚Äĺ)‚ąß(y‚Äĺ‚ą®z‚ą®w)\varphi = \exists x \forall y \exists z \forall w : (x \vee y \vee \overline{z}) \wedge (\overline{x} \vee \overline{y} \vee \overline{w}) \wedge (\overline{y} \vee z \vee w)ŌÜ=‚ąÉx‚ąÄy‚ąÉz‚ąÄw:(x‚ą®y‚ą®z)‚ąß(x‚ą®y‚Äč‚ą®w)‚ąß(y‚Äč‚ą®z‚ą®w)

I will continue using this example formula. We obtain the following proof operator sequence:

‚ąÉxLx‚ąÄyLxLy‚ąÉzLxLyLz‚ąÄwLxLyLzLwPŌÜ=1\exists_x L_x \forall_y L_x L_y \exists_z L_x L_y L_z \forall_w L_x L_y L_z L_w P_{\varphi} = 1‚ąÉx‚ÄčLx‚Äč‚ąÄy‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚Äč‚ąÉz‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚Äč‚ąÄw‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚ÄčLw‚ÄčPŌÜ‚Äč=1

For this particular formula ŌÜ\varphiŌÜ, the value of the polynomial will always be 111 regardless of the choice of ppp, but from now on we will do all computations modulo p=17>16=2np = 17 > 16 = 2^np=17>16=2n. If we consider each operator in the above sequence separately and evaluate the part after that operator, then we obtain:

Lx‚ąÄyLxLy‚ąÉzLxLyLz‚ąÄwLxLyLzLwPŌÜ=1‚ąíx‚ąÄyLxLy‚ąÉzLxLyLz‚ąÄwLxLyLzLwPŌÜ=1‚ąíx2LxLy‚ąÉzLxLyLz‚ąÄwLxLyLzLwPŌÜ=‚ąí2xy+x+1Ly‚ąÉzLxLyLz‚ąÄwLxLyLzLwPŌÜ=‚ąí2xy+x+1\begin{aligned}
L_x \forall_y L_x L_y \exists_z L_x L_y L_z \forall_w L_x L_y L_z L_w P_{\varphi} &= 1 - x \\
\forall_y L_x L_y \exists_z L_x L_y L_z \forall_w L_x L_y L_z L_w P_{\varphi} &= 1 - x^2 \\
L_x L_y \exists_z L_x L_y L_z \forall_w L_x L_y L_z L_w P_{\varphi} &= -2 x y + x + 1 \\
L_y \exists_z L_x L_y L_z \forall_w L_x L_y L_z L_w P_{\varphi} &= -2 x y + x + 1
\end{aligned}Lx‚Äč‚ąÄy‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚Äč‚ąÉz‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚Äč‚ąÄw‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚ÄčLw‚ÄčPŌÜ‚Äč‚ąÄy‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚Äč‚ąÉz‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚Äč‚ąÄw‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚ÄčLw‚ÄčPŌÜ‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚Äč‚ąÉz‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚Äč‚ąÄw‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚ÄčLw‚ÄčPŌÜ‚ÄčLy‚Äč‚ąÉz‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚Äč‚ąÄw‚ÄčLx‚ÄčLy‚ÄčLz‚ÄčLw‚ÄčPŌÜ‚Äč‚Äč=1‚ąíx=1‚ąíx2=‚ąí2xy+x+1=‚ąí2xy+x+1‚Äč

Here, I have included only the first 4 polynomials. It follows that the prover must in the first round send h(x1)=1‚ąíx1h(x_1) = 1 - x_1h(x1‚Äč)=1‚ąíx1‚Äč followed by h(x1)=1‚ąíx12h(x_1) = 1 - x_1^2h(x1‚Äč)=1‚ąíx12‚Äč in the second round. Then the verifier will change the polynomial ggg and assign a randomly chosen but fixed value to the xxx variable. If, for example, the verifier chooses x:=a=10x := a = 10x:=a=10, then it means that in the third round the verifier would need to send h(x1)=(‚ąí2)‚čÖ10‚čÖy+10+1=‚ąí3y‚ąí6h(x_1) = (-2) \cdot 10 \cdot y + 10 + 1  = -3y - 6h(x1‚Äč)=(‚ąí2)‚čÖ10‚čÖy+10+1=‚ąí3y‚ąí6. More generally, the s=hs = hs=h polynomial the prover must send to be able to convince the verifier that ŌÜ‚ąąTQBF‚Ā°\varphi \in \operatorname{TQBF}ŌÜ‚ąąTQBF is essentially the intermediate evaluation of the proof operator sequence, with values the verifier chose substituted for all variables except the current one. This is exactly the reason why it is essential that we ensure that the intermediate polynomials in the proof operator sequence all have low degree ‚ÄĒ otherwise the prover might not be able to come up with the s=hs = hs=h polynomial of degree polynomial in the size of ŌÜ\varphiŌÜ.

The following interactive transcript gets produces for ŌÜ\varphiŌÜ, of which I am including only first 4 rounds here:

Working modulo prime p = 17
[V]: Asking prover to send value of the entire polynomial
[P]: Value = 1 =: c
------------------------------
Starting round 1. Current operator: E_{x}
[V]: Random choices: none
[V]: Asking prover to send s(x) = h(x)
[P]: Sending s(x) = 1 - x
[P]: deg(s(x)) = 1
[V]: s(0) + s(1) = 1, expecting to be equal to c = 1
[V]: Chose a = 13 for variable x
[V]: Random choices: x := 13
[V]: s(a) = 5 =: c
------------------------------
Starting round 2. Current operator: L_{x}
[V]: Asking prover to send s(x) = h(x)
[P]: Sending s(x) = 1 - x**2
[P]: deg(s(x)) = 2
[V]: a_1 * s_1 + (1 - a_1) * s_0 = 5, expecting to be equal to c = 5
[V]: Re-chose a = 10 for variable x (while linearizing it)
[V]: Random choices: x := 10
[V]: s(a) = 3 =: c
------------------------------
Starting round 3. Current operator: A_{y}
[V]: Random choices: x := 10
[V]: Asking prover to send s(y) = h(y)
[P]: Sending s(y) = -3*y - 6
[P]: deg(s(y)) = 1
[V]: s(0) * s(1) = 3, expecting to be equal to c = 3
[V]: Chose a = 10 for variable y
[V]: Random choices: x := 10, y := 10
[V]: s(a) = 15 =: c
------------------------------
Starting round 4. Current operator: L_{x}
[V]: Asking prover to send s(x) = h(x)
[P]: Sending s(x) = 1 - 2*x
[P]: deg(s(x)) = 1
[V]: a_1 * s_1 + (1 - a_1) * s_0 = 15, expecting to be equal to c = 15
[V]: Re-chose a = 15 for variable x (while linearizing it)
[V]: Random choices: x := 15, y := 10
[V]: s(a) = 5 =: c


And running the tool in rendering mode produces the following visualization of the protocol applied to ŌÜ\varphiŌÜ:





The animation consists of the following parts:


  At the very top, the sequence of proof operators is located. In every round of communication, the proof operator corresponding to the beginning of the polynomial being considered, namely ggg, is highlighted with a yellow surrounding rectangle.
  Below the sequence of proof operators, the values that the verifier has assigned to variables so far, are displayed.
  In the center of the animation, the sequence of proof operators is visualized with a tree. This visualization allows us to very clearly see, in particular, the connection between ccc and the sequence of proof operators, or, in other words, value of what part of the polynomial is currently being verified.
  In the bottom corners, the V and P stand for verifier and prover, respectively. Between these labels, the communication is visualized. In particular, it is visualized which messages both parties exchange and how the verifier checks that what the prover says is correct, in accordance with the protocol defined above.


Correctness of the protocol

Finally, to make the description of the protocol in this post complete, I would like to explain in my own words why the protocol defined above is correct. The completeness property of IP‚Ā°\operatorname{IP}IP holds obviously ‚ÄĒ if ŌÜ‚ąąTQBF‚Ā°\varphi \in \operatorname{TQBF}ŌÜ‚ąąTQBF, then an honest prover will be able to convince the verifier no matter how the verifier tosses coins.

For the soundness analysis, we will first show the following lemma:

Lemma: Let ggg be a polynomial, c,a1,‚Ä¶,ak‚ąąFpc, a_1, \dots, a_k \in \mathbb{F}_pc,a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ak‚Äč‚ąąFp‚Äč and suppose that we are checking whether g(a1,‚Ä¶,ak)=cg(a_1, \dots, a_k) = cg(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ak‚Äč)=c using the operator composition check protocol, but actually g(a1,‚Ä¶,ak)‚Č†cg(a_1, \dots, a_k) \neq cg(a1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ak‚Äč)ÓÄ†‚Äč=c. Then the probability of either


  the verifier rejecting the proof in that very round (at step 4), or
  the verifier forcing the prover to continue lying in the next round, that is, re-running the protocol to verify s(a)=g‚Ä≤(a,a2,‚Ä¶,ak)s(a) = g'(a, a_2, \dots, a_k)s(a)=g‚Ä≤(a,a2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ak‚Äč) when it is actually the case that s(a)‚Č†g‚Ä≤(a,a2,‚Ä¶,ak)s(a) \neq g'(a, a_2, \dots, a_k)s(a)ÓÄ†‚Äč=g‚Ä≤(a,a2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ak‚Äč)


is at least 1‚ąíd/p1 - d/p1‚ąíd/p, where d=deg‚Ā°(h(x1))d = \deg(h(x_1))d=deg(h(x1‚Äč)).

Proof: If the prover sends s=hs = hs=h, then, depending on the current proof operator, one of the checks in step 4 will fail and the verifier will therefore reject with probability 1‚Č•1‚ąíd/p1 \ge 1-d/p1‚Č•1‚ąíd/p. On the other hand, if the prover sends s‚Č†hs \neq hsÓÄ†‚Äč=h, then the polynomial s(x1)‚ąíh(x1)‚Č†0s(x_1) - h(x_1) \neq 0s(x1‚Äč)‚ąíh(x1‚Äč)ÓÄ†‚Äč=0 has degree at most ddd. This means that s‚ąíhs - hs‚ąíh has at most ddd roots, i.e., there exist at most ddd values a‚ąąFpa \in \mathbb{F}_pa‚ąąFp‚Äč such that s(a)=h(a)s(a) = h(a)s(a)=h(a). In other words, Pr‚Ā°[s(a)=h(a)]‚Č§d/p\Pr[s(a) = h(a)] \le d/pPr[s(a)=h(a)]‚Č§d/p. Since the verifier uses the value c=s(a)c = s(a)c=s(a) in the next round, it holds that

Pr‚Ā°[s(a)‚Č†g‚Ä≤(a,a2,‚Ä¶,ak)]=Pr‚Ā°[s(a)‚Č†h(a)]‚Č•1‚ąíd/p\Pr[s(a) \neq g'(a, a_2, \dots, a_k)] = \Pr[s(a) \neq h(a)] \ge 1 - d/pPr[s(a)ÓÄ†‚Äč=g‚Ä≤(a,a2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,ak‚Äč)]=Pr[s(a)ÓÄ†‚Äč=h(a)]‚Č•1‚ąíd/p

This completes the proof of the lemma.

Now suppose that ŌÜ‚ąČTQBF‚Ā°\varphi \notin \operatorname{TQBF}ŌÜ‚ąą/‚ÄčTQBF. Then it is clear that the probability of the verifier not being fooled by the prover is at least the probability of the prover being forced to lie again and again, until he is either caught at some (possibly last) round. By applying the above lemma inductively, we get:

Pr‚Ā°[Verifier¬†rejects¬†or¬†prover¬†continues¬†lying¬†after¬†k¬†rounds]‚Č•(1‚ąídp)k\Pr[\text{Verifier rejects or prover continues lying after } k \text{ rounds}] \ge \Big(1 - \frac{d}{p}\Big)^kPr[Verifier¬†rejects¬†or¬†prover¬†continues¬†lying¬†after¬†k¬†rounds]‚Č•(1‚ąípd‚Äč)k

Since k‚Č§2‚čÖn2‚ąąO(n2)k \le 2 \cdot n^2 \in O(n^2)k‚Č§2‚čÖn2‚ąąO(n2), p>2np > 2^np>2n and d‚Č§cnc+cd \le cn^c+cd‚Č§cnc+c for some constant ccc, it holds that

Pr‚Ā°[Verifier¬†rejects]‚Č•(1‚ąídp)2n2‚Č•(1‚ąícnc+c2n)2n2\Pr[\text{Verifier rejects}] \ge \Big(1 - \frac{d}{p}\Big)^{2n^2} \ge \Big(1 - \frac{cn^c+c}{2^n}\Big)^{2n^2}Pr[Verifier¬†rejects]‚Č•(1‚ąípd‚Äč)2n2‚Č•(1‚ąí2ncnc+c‚Äč)2n2

We can calculate the limit of this probability using L‚ÄôH√īpital‚Äôs rule:

lim‚ÄČinf‚Ā°n‚Üí‚ąěPr‚Ā°[Verifier¬†rejects]‚Č•lim‚Ā°n‚Üí‚ąě(1‚ąícnc+c2n)2n2=1\liminf_{n \to \infty}{\Pr[\text{Verifier rejects}]} \ge \lim_{n \to \infty}{\Big(1 - \frac{cn^c+c}{2^n}\Big)^{2n^2}} = 1n‚Üí‚ąěliminf‚ÄčPr[Verifier¬†rejects]‚Č•n‚Üí‚ąělim‚Äč(1‚ąí2ncnc+c‚Äč)2n2=1

By the definition of the limit, this means that for any 0<őĶ‚Č§10 < \varepsilon \le 10<őĶ‚Č§1 there exists a fixed n‚ąąNn \in \mathbb{N}n‚ąąN such that for all QBF sentences ŌÜ\varphiŌÜ with at least nnn variables the probability of the verifier rejecting ŌÜ‚ąČTQBF‚Ā°\varphi \notin \operatorname{TQBF}ŌÜ‚ąą/‚ÄčTQBF is at least 1‚ąíőĶ1 - \varepsilon1‚ąíőĶ. This in particular implies the soundness requirement of the IP‚Ā°\operatorname{IP}IP complexity class, thus completing the proof of the correctness of the protocol.

documents.title: Computing square roots modulo a prime with the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm
documents.content:
This post is about the problem of computing square roots modulo a prime number, a well-known problem in algebra and number theory. Nowadays multiple highly-efficient algorithms have been developed to solve this problem, e.g. Tonelli-Shanks, Cipolla‚Äôs algorithms. In this post we will focus on one of the most prominent algorithms, namely the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm, which is often described in a rather complicated way in the literature, although its derivation and the idea behind it is actually simple and elegant. The goal of this post is to showcase this beauty of the theory and to then show how the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm can be implemented in Python. We also discuss some interesting connections of this problem to cryptography.

Preliminaries

Formally, the problem of computing square roots modulo a prime is to solve the congruence

b2‚Č°amod‚ÄČ‚ÄČpb^2 \equiv a \mod pb2‚Č°amodp

for bbb, given an odd prime number ppp and a‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąóa \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*a‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó, which is also called a quadratic residue if the congruence above holds. We first discuss some theory we will rely on in the next section about the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm itself.

Let qpq_pqp‚Äč be the squaring endomorphism defined as follows

qp:(Z/pZ)‚ąó‚Üí(Z/pZ)‚ąóa‚Ü¶a2\begin{aligned}
q_p : (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^* &\rightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^* \\
a &\mapsto a^2
\end{aligned}qp‚Äč:(Z/pZ)‚ąóa‚Äč‚Üí(Z/pZ)‚ąó‚Ü¶a2‚Äč

(recall that an endomorphism is a homomorphism of an algebraic structure to itself, it is an easy exercise to verify that qpq_pqp‚Äč is indeed an endomorphism). Observe that ker‚Ā°(qp)={1,‚ąí1}\ker(q_p) = \{1, -1\}ker(qp‚Äč)={1,‚ąí1}, because every element of the kernel is a root of the x2‚ąí1=(x‚ąí1)‚čÖ(x+1)x^2 - 1 = (x - 1) \cdot (x + 1)x2‚ąí1=(x‚ąí1)‚čÖ(x+1) polynomial, which cannot have more than two roots because of its degree, meaning that 111 and ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1 are its only roots. Let

Qp:=im‚Ā°(qp)={x2‚ą£x‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó}Q_p := \operatorname{im}(q_p) = \{x^2 \mid x \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*\}Qp‚Äč:=im(qp‚Äč)={x2‚ą£x‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó}

be the set of all squares modulo ppp. In the following lemma, we derive a formula for the exact number of squares modulo ppp:

Lemma: Let ppp be an odd prime. Then ‚ą£Qp‚ą£=p‚ąí12\st Q_p\st = \frac{p-1}{2}‚ą£Qp‚Äč‚ą£=2p‚ąí1‚Äč.

Proof: By the fundamental homomorphism theorem, there exists an isomorphism

(Z/pZ)‚ąó/ker‚Ā°(qp)‚ČÖim‚Ā°(qp)=Qp(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^* / \ker(q_p) \cong \operatorname{im}(q_p) = Q_p(Z/pZ)‚ąó/ker(qp‚Äč)‚ČÖim(qp‚Äč)=Qp‚Äč

Since all cosets have equal cardinality,

‚ą£Qp‚ą£=‚ą£im‚Ā°(qp)‚ą£=‚ą£(Z/pZ)‚ąó‚ą£‚ą£ker‚Ā°(qp)‚ą£=p‚ąí12\st Q_p\st = \st \operatorname{im}(q_p)\st = 
\frac{\st (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*\st}{\st \ker(q_p)\st} =
\frac{p - 1}{2}‚ą£Qp‚Äč‚ą£=‚ą£im(qp‚Äč)‚ą£=‚ą£ker(qp‚Äč)‚ą£‚ą£(Z/pZ)‚ąó‚ą£‚Äč=2p‚ąí1‚Äč

This completes the proof.

Another major tool for characterizing squares modulo ppp is the Legendre symbol defined as follows

(ap):={0a‚Č°0mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČp1a‚Č°Őł0mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČp¬†and¬†a¬†is¬†a¬†square¬†modulo¬†p‚ąí1otherwise\Big(\frac{a}{p}\Big) := \begin{cases}
0 & a \equiv 0 \mod p \\
1 & a \not\equiv 0 \mod p \text{ and } a \text{ is a square modulo } p \\
-1 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}(pa‚Äč):=‚é©‚é™‚é™‚é®‚é™‚é™‚éß‚Äč01‚ąí1‚Äča‚Č°0modpaÓÄ†‚Äč‚Č°0modp¬†and¬†a¬†is¬†a¬†square¬†modulo¬†potherwise‚Äč

where a‚ąąZa \in \mathbb{Z}a‚ąąZ and ppp is a prime. If a‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąóa \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*a‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó, then we can think of (ap)\big(\frac{a}{p}\big)(pa‚Äč) as of an ‚Äúindicator‚ÄĚ variable outputting 111 if b2‚Č°amod‚ÄČ‚ÄČpb^2 \equiv a \mod pb2‚Č°amodp holds for some bbb and ‚ąí1-1‚ąí1 otherwise. In fact, the Legendre symbol can be computed efficiently via the Euler‚Äôs criterion formulated in the following theorem.

Theorem (Euler): Let a‚ąąZa \in \mathbb{Z}a‚ąąZ and ppp is an odd prime. Then:

(ap)‚Č°ap‚ąí12mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČp\Big(\frac{a}{p}\Big) \equiv a^{\frac{p-1}{2}} \mod p(pa‚Äč)‚Č°a2p‚ąí1‚Äčmodp

Proof: If a‚Č°0mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČpa \equiv 0 \mod pa‚Č°0modp, then the statement holds trivially. We thus focus only on the case when a‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąóa \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*a‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó. By Fermat‚Äôs little theorem, it holds that

(ap‚ąí12)2‚Č°ap‚ąí1‚Č°1mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČp\Big(a^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\Big)^2 \equiv a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \mod p(a2p‚ąí1‚Äč)2‚Č°ap‚ąí1‚Č°1modp

which is equivalent to

(ap‚ąí12)2‚ąí1‚Č°0mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČp\Big(a^{\frac{p-1}{2}}\Big)^2 - 1 \equiv 0 \mod p(a2p‚ąí1‚Äč)2‚ąí1‚Č°0modp

meaning that

Computing square roots modulo a prime

First observe that since ppp is a prime, Z/pZ\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}Z/pZ is a field and hence the polynomial x2‚ąíax^2 - ax2‚ąía has exactly two roots. It follows that it suffices to compute just one root of aaa, because it is trivial to obtain the other one by simply performing negation modulo ppp. From now on, all computation will be modulo ppp if not stated otherwise. We also assume that a‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąóa \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*a‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó, or, equivalently, that a‚Č†0a \neq 0aÓÄ†‚Äč=0, because it is trivial to compute the square root of zero.

The core idea behind the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm is to make use of the fact that if am=1a^m = 1am=1 for some odd m‚ąąNm \in \mathbb{N}m‚ąąN, then

(am+12)2=am+1=a\Big(a^{\frac{m+1}{2}}\Big)^2 = a^{m+1} = a(a2m+1‚Äč)2=am+1=a

and hence am+12a^{\frac{m+1}{2}}a2m+1‚Äč is a square root of aaa. By the Euler‚Äôs theorem of the previous section above, we know that ap‚ąí12=1a^{\frac{p-1}{2}} = 1a2p‚ąí1‚Äč=1 holds if and only if aaa is a square modulo ppp: if ap‚ąí12=‚ąí1a^{\frac{p-1}{2}} = -1a2p‚ąí1‚Äč=‚ąí1, then the algorithm can directly identify that the given number is not a square and terminate accordingly. Thus, intuitively m=p‚ąí12m = \frac{p-1}{2}m=2p‚ąí1‚Äč is a ‚Äúgood‚ÄĚ initial value to start with while searching for an odd m‚ąąNm \in \mathbb{N}m‚ąąN satisfying am=1a^m = 1am=1. The remaining ideas are best shown by looking at how the algorithm works on a concrete example.

The Tonelli-Shanks algorithm

We now state the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm in general:


  
    Set m:=p‚ąí12m := \frac{p-1}{2}m:=2p‚ąí1‚Äč. If am=‚ąí1a^m = -1am=‚ąí1, then return ‚Äúnot a square‚ÄĚ, because aaa is not a square modulo ppp by Euler‚Äôs theorem (see above) and ama^mam is equal to the Legendre symbol.
  
  
    Otherwise we have that am=1a^m = 1am=1. Set k:=1k := 1k:=1, so that m=p‚ąí12km = \frac{p-1}{2^k}m=2kp‚ąí1‚Äč
  
  
    At this point we have the invariant that am=1a^m = 1am=1 for m=p‚ąí12km = \frac{p-1}{2^k}m=2kp‚ąí1‚Äč. While mmm is even and am=1a^m = 1am=1, set k:=k+1k := k + 1k:=k+1 and update m:=p‚ąí12km := \frac{p-1}{2^k}m:=2kp‚ąí1‚Äč.
  
  
    If am=1a^m = 1am=1, then the square root of aaa is am+12a^{\frac{m+1}{2}}a2m+1‚Äč by the above discussion, since mmm is odd.
  
  
    Otherwise it holds that am=‚ąí1a^m = -1am=‚ąí1 for m=p‚ąí12km = \frac{p-1}{2^k}m=2kp‚ąí1‚Äč, where k‚Č•2k \ge 2k‚Č•2. Choose an arbitrary non-square b‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó‚ąĖQpb \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^* \setminus Q_pb‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó‚ąĖQp‚Äč by repeatedly picking b‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąób \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*b‚ąą(Z/pZ)‚ąó uniformly at random until (bp)=bp‚ąí12=‚ąí1(\frac{b}{p}) = b^{\frac{p-1}{2}} = -1(pb‚Äč)=b2p‚ąí1‚Äč=‚ąí1.
  
  
    Run the same algorithm recursively from step 3 with the variable aaa set to a‚Ä≤:=a‚čÖb2k‚ąí1a' := a \cdot b^{2^{k-1}}a‚Ä≤:=a‚čÖb2k‚ąí1 and other variables left unchanged. This establishes the invariant of step 3, because
  


1=am‚Źü‚ąí1‚čÖbp‚ąí12‚Źü‚ąí1=ap‚ąí12k‚Źü‚ąí1‚čÖbp‚ąí12‚Źü‚ąí1=ap‚ąí12k‚čÖ(b2k‚ąí1)p‚ąí12k=(a‚čÖb2k‚ąí1)p‚ąí12k=(a‚čÖb2k‚ąí1‚Źüa‚Ä≤)m\begin{aligned}
1 = \underbrace{a^m}_{-1} \cdot \underbrace{b^{\frac{p-1}{2}}}_{-1} =
\underbrace{a^{\frac{p-1}{2^k}}}_{-1} \cdot

a^{\frac{p-1}{2^k}} \cdot \Big(b^{2^{k-1}}\Big)^{\frac{p-1}{2^k}} \\
&= \Big(a \cdot b^{2^{k-1}}\Big)^{\frac{p-1}{2^k}}
= \Big(\underbrace{a \cdot b^{2^{k-1}}}_{a'}\Big)^m

  Let a‚Ä≤\sqrt{a'}a‚Ä≤‚Äč be the square root of a‚Ä≤a'a‚Ä≤ obtained from the recursive call. We are interested in computing a\sqrt{a}a‚Äč such that (a)2=a(\sqrt{a})^2 = a(a‚Äč)2=a (interpret a‚Ä≤\sqrt{a'}a‚Ä≤‚Äč and a\sqrt{a}a‚Äč as new variables, not as operations). Observe that


(a‚Ä≤)2=a‚Ä≤=a‚čÖb2k‚ąí1=(a)2‚čÖb2k‚ąí1\big(\sqrt{a'}\big)^2 = a' = a \cdot b^{2^{k-1}} 
= (\sqrt{a})^2 \cdot b^{2^{k-1}}(a‚Ä≤‚Äč)2=a‚Ä≤=a‚čÖb2k‚ąí1=(a‚Äč)2‚čÖb2k‚ąí1


  Bringing (a)2(\sqrt{a})^2(a‚Äč)2 to the left side yields


(a)2=(a‚Ä≤)2‚čÖb‚ąí2k‚ąí1=(a‚Ä≤‚čÖb‚ąí2k‚ąí2)2(\sqrt{a})^2 = \big(\sqrt{a'}\big)^2 \cdot b^{-2^{k-1}}
= \Big(\sqrt{a'} \cdot b^{-2^{k-2}}\Big)^2(a‚Äč)2=(a‚Ä≤‚Äč)2‚čÖb‚ąí2k‚ąí1=(a‚Ä≤‚Äč‚čÖb‚ąí2k‚ąí2)2


  Note that we have used the fact that k‚Č•2k \ge 2k‚Č•2 here. It follows that a‚Ä≤‚čÖb‚ąí2k‚ąí2\sqrt{a'} \cdot b^{-2^{k-2}}a‚Ä≤‚Äč‚čÖb‚ąí2k‚ąí2 is a square root of aaa modulo ppp.


We now argue why this algorithm always terminates and is correct. Observe that mmm is changed throughout the algorithm in a way which ensures that am‚ąą{1,‚ąí1}a^m \in \{1, -1\}am‚ąą{1,‚ąí1} always holds. This is the case, because 1,‚ąí11, -11,‚ąí1 are the only square roots of 111 modulo ppp and in every step we divide the exponent mmm only by two. The case when am=‚ąí1a^m = -1am=‚ąí1 is reduced to the case when am=1a^m = 1am=1 but for a different aaa, such that the square root for the original aaa can be recovered from the square root of the new aaa. Finally, observe that the algorithm either terminates directly because am=1a^m = 1am=1 holds for an odd mmm, or kkk gets increased by at least one and hence mmm gets divided by a power of two greater than one. This guarantees termination, because iterative division of mmm by two will at some point yield an odd number whereupon the algorithm will terminate.

We now make a few additional remarks about the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm.


  
    If p‚ąí12\frac{p-1}{2}2p‚ąí1‚Äč is odd or, equivalently, if p‚Č°3mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČ4p \equiv 3 \mod 4p‚Č°3mod4, then the algorithm will not make any recursive calls and output the square root am+12=ap+14a^{\frac{m+1}{2}} = a^{\frac{p+1}{4}}a2m+1‚Äč=a4p+1‚Äč of aaa.
  
  
    The loop in step 3 of the algorithm can be optimized by replacing it with a binary search algorithm for finding an mmm such that am=‚ąí1a^m = -1am=‚ąí1. If during such binary search, we have am=1a^m = 1am=1, then we continue searching for smaller values of mmm. If am‚ąČ{1,‚ąí1}a^m \notin \{1, -1\}am‚ąą/‚Äč{1,‚ąí1}, then this means that we have divided mmm too many times, so it follows that binary search is to be continued for larger values of mmm. Although this optimization is beautiful theoretically, in practice it may only give a very tiny performance gain, if any.
  
  
    The non-square bbb used in the algorithm needs to be found and computed only once. During every recursive call, the same value of bbb can be reused to deal with the am=‚ąí1a^m = -1am=‚ąí1 situation. This implies that b‚ąí1b^{-1}b‚ąí1 needs to be computed only once, so the computation of the a‚Ä≤‚čÖb‚ąí2k‚ąí2\sqrt{a'} \cdot b^{-2^{k-2}}a‚Ä≤‚Äč‚čÖb‚ąí2k‚ąí2 value can be optimized.
  
  
    As regards the expected time needed to find a non-square bbb via guessing, since exactly half of the elements of (Z/pZ)‚ąó(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*(Z/pZ)‚ąó are non-squares, two is the expected number of times we need to generate bbb uniformly at random and to test whether it is a non-square or not.
  
  
    The expected running time of the algorithm is O(log‚Ā°(p)2)O(\log(p)^2)O(log(p)2), if we assume that multiplication modulo ppp is a constant-time operation.
  


Implementation in Python

In this section I would like to show how the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm discussed above can be implemented in Python. We will need the following utility algorithms:


  
    The extended Euclidean algorithm for computing inverses modulo ppp
  
  
    Fast exponentiation (by squaring) algorithm
  


I will reuse the implementation of the extended Euclidean algorithm I described and proved correct in this post. We are now ready to dive into the code:

# file: "tonellishanks.py"
import random


def power_modulo(a: int, b: int, n: int) -> int:
    """ Computes a ^ b mod n """
    result = 1
    while b != 0:
        if b % 2 == 1:
            # b odd
            result = (result * a) % n
        a = (a * a) % n
        b >>= 1
    return result


def extended_gcd(a: int, b: int) -> (int, int, int):
    # optional check
    if a == 0:
        return b, 0, 1

    # without this check the first iteration will divide by zero
    if b == 0:
        return a, 1, 0

    un_prev = 1
    vn_prev = 0
    un_cur = 0
    vn_cur = 1

    while True:
        qn = a // b
        new_r = a % b
        a = b
        b = new_r

        if b == 0:
            return a, un_cur, vn_cur

        # Update coefficients
        un_new = un_prev - qn * un_cur
        vn_new = vn_prev - qn * vn_cur

        # Shift coefficients
        un_prev = un_cur
        vn_prev = vn_cur
        un_cur = un_new
        vn_cur = vn_new


def inverse_modulo(a: int, n: int) -> int:
    _, b, _ = extended_gcd(a, n)
    return b % n


def legendre_symbol(a: int, p: int, /) -> int:
    return power_modulo(a, (p - 1) >> 1, p)


def _choose_b(p: int, /) -> int:
    b = 2
    while legendre_symbol(b, p) == 1:
        b = random.randrange(2, p)
    return b


def _tonelli_shanks_recursive(a: int, k: int, p: int, b: int, b_inverse: int, /):
    """
    Computes a square root of a modulo prime p
    :param a: the number to take the square root of
    :param k: positive integer, such that a^m = 1 (mod p) where m = (p-1)/(2^k)
    :param p: odd prime p modulo which we are working
    :param b: an arbitrary non-square modulo p
    :param b_inverse: the inverse of b modulo p, i.e., b * b_inverse = 1 (mod p)
    :return: one of the square roots of a modulo p (the other can be obtained via negation modulo p)
    """

    m = (p - 1) >> k
    a_m = 1

    while m % 2 == 0 and a_m == 1:
        m >>= 1
        k += 1
        a_m = power_modulo(a, m, p)

    if a_m == p - 1:
        # a^m = -1 (mod p)
        b_power = 1 << (k - 1)
        b_power_half = 1 << (k - 2)
        a_next = (a * power_modulo(b, b_power, p)) % p
        a_next_root = _tonelli_shanks_recursive(a_next, k, p, b, b_inverse)
        a_root = a_next_root * power_modulo(b_inverse, b_power_half, p)
        return a_root % p

    # we now handle the case when m is odd
    # this case is easy, a^((m+1)/2) is a square root of a
    return power_modulo(a, (m + 1) >> 1, p)


def tonelli_shanks(a: int, p: int, /) -> int | None:
    """
    Computes a square root of a modulo prime p
    :param a: the number to take the square root of
    :param p: odd prime p modulo which we are working
    :return: one of the square roots of a modulo p (the other can be obtained via negation modulo p)
    """

    if legendre_symbol(a, p) != 1:
        # a is not not a square modulo p
        return None

    b = _choose_b(p)
    b_inverse = inverse_modulo(b, p)
    return _tonelli_shanks_recursive(a, 1, p, b, b_inverse)


Recall that performing a left bitwise shift (<<) is essentially a fast way to multiply the left operand with two raised to the right operand value‚Äôs power. Similarly, a right bitwise shift (>>) divides the left operand by two raised to the right operand value‚Äôs power.

Running the implementation on the example of the previous section, that is, for a=8a = 8a=8 and p=17p = 17p=17 yields the expected correct roots:

$ python3 -i tonellishanks.py
>>> tonelli_shanks(8, 17)
5
>>> tonelli_shanks(8, 17)
12


Note: The above implementation is a simplified version of my thoroughly tested implementation of the algorithm available in this GitHub repository.

Related hard problems

I find the problem of computing square roots modulo a prime especially interesting because it is in some sense very ‚Äúclose‚ÄĚ to problems where there are very good reasons to believe that they are hard in the complexity-theoretic sense. For example, the same problem is not known to be efficiently solvable if we are working modulo a Blum number, that is, if we are interested in solving

b2‚Č°amod‚ÄČ‚ÄČp‚čÖqb^2 \equiv a \mod p \cdot qb2‚Č°amodp‚čÖq

for bbb, where p‚Č†qp \neq qpÓÄ†‚Äč=q are prime numbers such that p‚Č°q‚Č°3mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČ4p \equiv q \equiv 3 \mod 4p‚Č°q‚Č°3mod4. Moreover, this modified problem is at least as hard as the problem of factoring Blum numbers, because given an oracle for computing the four square roots for aaa, one could factor a given Blum number n=p‚čÖqn = p \cdot qn=p‚čÖq efficiently as follows:


  
    Choose a number x‚ąą(Z/nZ)‚ąóx \in (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^*x‚ąą(Z/nZ)‚ąó uniformly at random (can be implemented by simply choosing a random number xxx between 222 and n‚ąí1n - 1n‚ąí1 inclusively, because if x‚ąČ(Z/nZ)‚ąóx \notin (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^*x‚ąą/‚Äč(Z/nZ)‚ąó, then we have already effectively guessed the factor 1<gcd‚Ā°(x,n)<n1 < \gcd(x, n) < n1<gcd(x,n)<n of nnn)
  
  
    Use the (hypothetical) oracle to compute some square root rrr of x2x^2x2 modulo nnn.
  
  
    If r‚Č°xmod‚ÄČ‚ÄČnr \equiv x \mod nr‚Č°xmodn or r‚Č°‚ąíxmod‚ÄČ‚ÄČnr \equiv -x \mod nr‚Č°‚ąíxmodn, then we have failed to factor nnn.
  
  
    Otherwise it holds that (r‚ąíx)‚čÖ(r+x)‚Č°0mod‚ÄČ‚ÄČn(r - x) \cdot (r + x) \equiv 0 \mod n(r‚ąíx)‚čÖ(r+x)‚Č°0modn and the factors of nnn are gcd‚Ā°(r‚ąíx,n)\gcd(r - x, n)gcd(r‚ąíx,n) and gcd‚Ā°(r+x,n)\gcd(r + x, n)gcd(r+x,n).
  


Since x‚ąą(Z/nZ)‚ąóx \in (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^*x‚ąą(Z/nZ)‚ąó is chosen at random and we only pass x2x^2x2 to the oracle, the oracle‚Äôs answer is independent of xxx. It is not hard to show that every quadratic residue modulo a Blum number has exactly 4 distinct roots (using the Chinese Remainder Theorem), two of them are xxx and ‚ąíx-x‚ąíx which do not yield a factor of nnn in the algorithm above. It follows that the probability of successfully factoring a number is 1/21/21/2, meaning that the algorithm above is a randomized polynomial-time Turing reduction. Moreover, the success probability of the above algorithm can actually be significantly amplified, but this is outside of the scope of this post. It is also worth noting that the above security reduction is useful in cryptography. For example, the Rabin cryptosystem‚Äôs security heavily relies on the assumption that computing square roots modulo a Blum number is computationally hard, which as we have shown above is implied by the assumption that factoring Blum numbers is hard, which is a plausible hardness assumption to date. If Rabin‚Äôs cryptosystem did all computations modulo a prime instead of a Blum number, then the cipher would be easily breakable, since it would then be possible to decrypt plaintexts using the Tonelli-Shanks algorithm.

documents.title: SDL Grapher
documents.content:
SdlGrapher allows you to plot graphs for mathematical functions with SDL 2.0 in C++.

Features


  Horizontal / vertical scrolling.
  Scaling with mouse wheel.
  No rendering if the math function returns NaN or Infinity.
  Movable axises. Screen => Math, Math => Screen unit converters.
  Automatically calculate scale and axis position based on interval of the math function.
  Pixel perfect rendering.

documents.title: BST
documents.description: Optimized binary search tree implementation in C++.
documents.content:
Note: Binary search trees are not balanced by definition. This implementation does not guarantee O(log n) complexity when searching or deleting.

Features

  Pretty good performance compared to many other implementations on the internet.
  No recursion except for debugging purposes.
  Node-based on-delete balancing is supported. If you don‚Äôt need it, you can easily disable it.
  Unique / non-unique element insertion.
  2 deletion methods are supported:
    
      With payload copying (efficient, when it‚Äôs size is less than 2 * sizeof(void*)).
      With pointer rearrangement.
    
  
  Debug tree printing with indentation.
  Lightweight, only one header file.

documents.title: Knife
documents.description: Knife is a tool that reads input grammar in BNF format and converts it to a few Java classes that can parse the given grammar through a simple interface.
documents.content:
Knife doesn‚Äôt require any external libraries or dependencies. All generation is done ahead-of-time. After generating the parsing classes you can just copy them into your project.

Also, as other good parser generation tools, knife uses itself to read the input grammar.

Features


  No runtime dependencies, knife generates pure Java code that can easily be ported to other JVM-based languages.
  Parsing is done using push-down automata without recursion.
  Knife uses an explicit API for accepting the token stream. It allows you to easily use knife with any (including your own) lexer. You can pause and resume parsing at any point. Parsing multiple token streams simultaneously is also possible.
  No complete parse-trees are being built during parsing. Reduction of the tree is done on-the-fly for performance. Optimized AST‚Äôs can be built during parsing with minimal overhead.
  If your grammar is left-recursive without A =>* A derivations (aka without cycles), knife will generate an equivalent grammar without left recursion for you.
  Syntax error recovery using panic mode approach without any additional performance overhead.

documents.title: Grammax
documents.description: Grammax takes a grammar in BNF format as an input and converts it to a Java-class that recognizes the language generated by the grammar. Formally speaking, this tool creates a left-to-right, rightmost derivation (LR) parser for a given grammar. That means that grammax parses the given string by constructing a reversed rightmost derivation of it.
documents.content:
Grammax doesn‚Äôt require any external libraries or dependencies. All generation is done ahead-of-time. After generating the parsing classes you can just copy them into your project.

Also, as other good parser generation tools, grammax uses itself to read the input grammar.

Features


  No runtime dependencies, only pure Java code is generated.
  Parsing is done using a push-down automation without recursion.
  Grammax uses an explicit API for accepting the token stream. It allows you to easily use the tool with any (including your own) lexer. You can pause and resume parsing at any point. Parsing multiple token streams simultaneously is also possible.
  Grammax supports simple lr and canonical lr parsing algorithms.
  Automatic warnings about possible right-recursion cycles that cause a lot of parsing stack memory consumption.
  Types can be assigned to terminals and non-terminals. The corresponding expressions are casted automatically.
  The %top statement allows inserting package and import automatically. Therefore grammax can be used in an automated pipeline.

documents.title: GarbageSet
documents.description: Set data structure with all operation is O(1), including initialization!
documents.content:
This is a set data structure implementation in C. It can be initialized in constant time what makes it different compared to typical set implementation.

Complexity

The following table gives an overview of what the datastructure is capable of. All the complexities are calculated assuming memory allocation is performed in O(1).


  
    
      Operation
      Description
      Complexity
    
  
  
    
      garbageset_init
      Initializes the data structure with the specified capacity.
      O(1)
    
    
      garbageset_isset
      Checks if there is an element at a specified index.
      O(1)
    
    
      garbageset_get
      Retrieves the element at a specified indexor returns null, if there is no element at that index.
      O(1)
    
    
      garbageset_write
      Writes a new element at the specified index or overwrites an old one if it was defined.
      O(1)
    
  


Space complexity

The space complexity of the data structure is in O(n). However, apart from storing the payload array itself the data structure requires additional space for redundancy checking purposes. This additional space is about 2*n*sizeof(index_t) bytes where n is the capacity and index_t is the type used for indexes. With this user-defined you can easily reduce the memory overhead.
